coredata.dc:title: Preparation of dual-emission polyurethane/carbon dots thermoresponsive composite films for colorimetric temperature sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Yingnan
Zhang, Xiaojie
Xiao, Lizhi
Yan, Ruyue
Xin, Jingwei
Yin, Chunxia
Jia, Yunxiao
Zhao, Yu
Xiao, Chunying
Zhang, Zhe
Song, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent films have received great interest for chemo-/bio-sensing applications owing to their distinct advantages over solution-based probes, including their good stability; ease of storage, transport, and recycling; tunable shape and size; and non-invasive nature. In this study, mechanically stable polyurethane/carbon dot (PU/CDs) composite films with dual-emission for ratiometric temperature sensing were prepared. The films exhibited good light transmissivity, flexibility, and ductility. The obtained PU/CDs hybrid films with dual emission exhibited temperature response and reversible properties from 10 °C to 90 °C. At the appropriate reactive ratio of CDs to PU, the fluorescent color of the films shifted over a relatively large range from blue to green to yellow over a large temperature range. These findings indicate that the stretchable hybrid PU/CDs composite films could potentially be applied for colorimetric detection over a large temperature range (10 °C–90 °C). These materials thus have high application value for macroscopic dual-light temperature detection as well as further development of polymer composite modification.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank for the support from Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, Chemistry and Materials Engineering College, Jiang Nan University . This work was supported by Jilin Province science and technology development plan (Grant No. 20180201060SF ), Ministry of science and technology (Grant No. 2018YFC1706603 ), Science and technology development project of Jilin Province (Grant No. 20170309007YY ), Jilin Provincial health special project (No. sczsy201605 ), Jilin Province bureau of quality and technical supervision project ( DBXM146-2016 , DBXM109-2017 , DBXM114-2017 , DBXM099-2019 ), and excellent talents training program of China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University (No. YXQ1-201906 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of prepared CDs. (b) Fluorescent emission images of aqueous CDs solution under 365 nm UV lamp at different temperatures (10 °C–90 °C). (c) and (d) Change of fluorescent spectra (Ex = ∼400 nm, Em = ∼541 nm) of aqueous CDs solution with temperature increasing from 10 °C to 90 °C and decreasing from 90 °C to 10 °C, respectively. (e) Change of fluorescent intensity of prepared CDs in water under multiple reversible temperature changes, alternating between 20 °C and 80 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

(a) TEM image of PU/CDs composite film diluted by one-twentieth (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 2%). (b) Corresponding emission spectra of PU film (blue line) at excitation of 350 nm, CDs (yellow line) at excitation of 400 nm, and PU/CDs composite films (red line) at central excitation of 375 nm. (c) Transmittance curve and ductility of PU/CDs composite film. The insert shows fluorescent images of the composite films at different stretching states (primitive film, stretching film, and recovery film) under 365 nm UV light at room temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Fluorescence images of PU/CDs composite films prepared using different CDs to PU mass ratios (PU only, 0.4%, 1%, 2%, and 4%) under 365-nm UV excitation at room temperature. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Fluorescence spectra (Ex = 375 nm) of the composite PU/CDs film (at the low weight ratio of CDs to PU of 0.4%) as a function of increasing temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C (a) and decreasing temperature from 90 °C to 10 °C (b). (c) Five recycle tests of PU/CDs composite under reversible temperature changes alternating between 20 °C and 80 °C. (d) CIE coordinate changes of the fluorescence of the PU/CDs composite film in the temperature range from 10 °C to 90 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Fluorescence spectra (Ex = 375 nm) of composite PU/CDs film (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 1%) as a function of increasing temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C (a) and decreasing temperature from 90 °C to 10 °C (b). (c) Five recycle tests of PU/CDs composite under reversible temperature changes alternating between 20 °C and 80 °C. (d) CIE coordinate changes of the fluorescence of the PU/CDs composite film in the temperature range from 10 °C to 90 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Fluorescence spectra (Ex = 375 nm) of composite PU/CDs film (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 2%) as a function of increasing temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C (a) and decreasing temperature from 90 °C to 10 °C (b). (c) Five recycle tests of PU/CDs composite under reversible temperature changes alternating between 20 °C and 80 °C. (d) CIE coordinate changes of fluorescence of PU/CDs composite film in temperature range from 10 °C to 90 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).

Fluorescence images of PU/CDs composite films with CDs to PU ratio of 1% under UV excitation of 365 nm at increasing temperatures from 10 °C to 90 °C. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online).
Schematic illustration of preparation of PU/CDs composite films for colorimetric temperature sensing.

head.ce:title: Preparation of dual-emission polyurethane/carbon dots thermoresponsive composite films for colorimetric temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun, 130117, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jilin Ginseng Academy, Changchun University of Chinese Medicine, Changchun, 130117, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Gynecology and Obstetrics, Changchun Obstetrics Gynecology Hospital, Changchun, Jilin, 130042, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Gynecology and Obstetrics, Changchun Obstetrics Gynecology Hospital, Changchun, Jilin, 130042, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed to the work equally and should be regarded as co-first author.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent films have received great interest for chemo-/bio-sensing applications owing to their distinct advantages over solution-based probes, including their good stability; ease of storage, transport, and recycling; tunable shape and size; and non-invasive nature. In this study, mechanically stable polyurethane/carbon dot (PU/CDs) composite films with dual-emission for ratiometric temperature sensing were prepared. The films exhibited good light transmissivity, flexibility, and ductility. The obtained PU/CDs hybrid films with dual emission exhibited temperature response and reversible properties from 10 °C to 90 °C. At the appropriate reactive ratio of CDs to PU, the fluorescent color of the films shifted over a relatively large range from blue to green to yellow over a large temperature range. These findings indicate that the stretchable hybrid PU/CDs composite films could potentially be applied for colorimetric detection over a large temperature range (10 °C–90 °C). These materials thus have high application value for macroscopic dual-light temperature detection as well as further development of polymer composite modification.

ce:section.ce:para: Temperature measurement is important for biomedical detection, industrial engineering, and environmental monitoring systems [1–21]. Precise temperature information is needed to guarantee the high efficiency and reliable operation of these systems [1–21]. Over the past decade, researchers have successfully developed several fluorescent probes for temperature detection, including quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), rare-earth materials, and metal nanoclusters [2–14]. Commonly, these fluorescent probes rely on changes of the luminous intensity for temperature detection. That is, the increasing nonradiation decay rate with increasing temperature leads to diminished fluorescent intensity [4,7–10]. However, fluorescent probes based on changes in intensity are susceptible to factors such as probe concentration, excitation efficiency, and poor selectivity, leading to measurement errors. Compared with single-emission fluorescent probes, colorimetric fluorescent probes with multiple fluorescent centers or that are made from multiple fluorescent materials have attracted much attention [5,11–14]. These devices are largely immune to the aforementioned external factors while also improving the accuracy of temperature tests.

Nevertheless, most fluorescent probes for temperature detection require the use of aqueous systems, which greatly limit their practical application [9–14]. Luminescent probes in aqueous solution are difficult to store, transport, and recycle. In addition, disposable probes lead to reagent consumption and environmental pollution. To address the above issues, multi-functional light-emitting sensitive thin films have been proposed [15–18]. They are much more stable with adjustable shape and size and are easy to design, transport, operate, and recycle. Recently, ratiometric sensitive films of two different band gaps, which can facilitate the detection of target materials, have attracted researcher interest [19–21]. Compared with traditional sensitive films based on fluorescence intensity change, these films can reduce the effect of external factors, such as excited state fluctuation, optical masking, and local inhomogeneity of the absolute intensity. Moreover, the dual light visual detections not only greatly increase the accuracy of the data but also effectively improve the test efficiency. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are only a few reports on the preparation of dual-emission films for ratiometric temperature sensing [19–21] (further details are provided in Table S1). Commonly, these films are prepared by doping various luminescent particles using a complicated preparation process. Although the temperature response range is relatively large, the temperature sensitivity is not high, with an unobvious visual change of the macroscopic films. For instance, Zhang et al. synthesized a dual-emitting fluorescent phosphor from cellulose nanocrystal-assisted CDs-grafted SrAl2O4, Eu2+, Dy3+ using a core–shell process. The phosphors were then reacted with coupling amino-silane and assembled with nanofibrillated cellulose skeletons to create flexible isotropic films, at temperature from 243 K to 383 K [21].

Because of the close relationship between the structure and properties in polyurethane (PU), this polymer is one of the most extensively used and promising multifunctional materials in the world [22–30]. Its unique mechanical, physical, biological, and chemical properties have attracted researcher interest for various applications. The properties of PU can be tailored to a specific application by reforming the raw materials and production process using appropriate methods [22–30]. Among the various types of PU, waterborne PU is an environmentally friendly material that has been widely used in the preparation of adhesives and coatings and for drug delivery and tissue engineering in recent years [24–29]. There is clearly a new opportunity and challenge in material science to introduce new properties to PU by adding different types of (nano)fillers to its molecular state [27–30]. The strong interaction between the waterborne PU matrix and waterborne filler could greatly promote the construction and design of new ideal PU composites.

As a new type of nanomaterial, fluorescent CDs have been the subject of rapid development in recent years [31–39]. Compared with traditional luminophore semiconductor nanocrystals, organic dyes, and metal nanoclusters, CDs possess the advantages of ultra-small size, good biocompatibility, high fluorescence intensity, good optical stability, and easy surface functionalization. They have been widely applied in the fields of biological imaging, photocatalysis, and sensing.

With the development of dual-emission films for accurate ratiometric temperature sensing in mind, herein, we first prepared new hybrid PU/CDs composite films with yellow-blue dual-light emission using a relatively simple method. Green-yellow fluorescent CDs were prepared from o-phenylenediamine (OPD) using a typical hydrothermal method [39]. In parallel, commercial waterborne PU and glycerol were added to deionized water to obtain solution-stable and uniform PU. Afterward, a certain amount of CDs was dripped into the PU solution depending on the target ratio. Finally, the mixed droplets were dried on a glass substrate, and hybrid PU/CDs composite films with yellow-blue dual-light emission were obtained (Scheme 1 ). The prepared films exhibited good light transmissivity, flexibility, ductility, and mechanical stability. The PU/CDs hybrid films with dual-emission capability exhibited excellent temperature response and reversible properties from 10 °C to 90 °C. Moreover, at an appropriate reactive ratio of CDs to PU, the fluorescent color of the films shifted over a relatively large range from blue to green to yellow over the temperature range of 10 °C–90 °C.

Transmission spectra were measured on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-3900, Japan), photoluminescence fluorescence spectra were measured on a spectrophotometer (Horiba Horiba JobinYvon Fluoromax 4C-L, France), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai GI F20 U-TWIN. An Olympus BM51 optical microscope was used to obtain light-field and fluorescence microscopy images with a MVC 1000 USB 2.0 used to capture the images.

All the chemical reagents used in the experiments were purchased commercially and did not require further purification. The glycerol and o-phenylenediamine used in the experiment were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Carboxylated PU was purchased from Shenzhen Jinxiu Waterproof Material Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China) [30]. All the aqueous solutions were configured using deionized water (DI).

The synthesis of the fluorescent CDs was performed following a previously reported procedure with minor modifications [35]. First, 400 mg of o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water in a beaker and stirred to obtain a uniform and transparent aqueous solution. Next, the o-phenylenediamine aqueous solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel high-pressure water vessel. The lid of the vessel was tightened, and the reaction vessel was placed in an oven at 150 °C for 2.5 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the reaction vessel was naturally cooled to room temperature to obtain an aqueous solution of CDs. The aqueous solution was centrifuged at 8500 rpm for 12 min. The resulting clarified CDs solution was filtered through a membrane with a pore size of 0.2 μm and then dialyzed into a dialysis bag for 36 h. The dialysate outside the bag was collected, and the CDs powder was obtained by evaporating the water using a rotary evaporator.

A total of 4 g of water-soluble PU and 1.5 mL of glycerin were added to a three-neck flask containing 25 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred thoroughly and then heated to 90 °C. After approximately 30 min, the mixed solution became uniform and was then cooled to room temperature before use. Depending on the desired weight ratio of PU to CDs, different volumes of CDs (0.08, 0.4, 0.8, or 1.6 mL) were added to the PU solution (to achieve a weight ratio of CDs to PU of 0.1%, 0.5%, 1%, 2%, and 4%, respectively). Then, the PU/CDs mixed solution was spread evenly on a clean glass substrate and dried at 50 °C for 2 h. Finally, hybrid PU/CDs composite films from different reaction ratios of CDs to PU with dual fluorescence emission were obtained.

First, we synthesized fluorescent CDs using OPD by a normal hydrothermal synthesis method. Fig. 1 (a) presents a TEM image of the prepared CDs. The size of CDs was relatively uniform and approximately 2–3 nm, and the dispersion was very good. No agglomeration was observed, which was beneficial for their further even dispersion in the PU film. Fig. 1 (b) presents fluorescence images of the purified CDs aqueous solution irradiated under a 365 nm UV lamp at different temperatures (10 °C–90 °C). Fig. S1 presents the UV–vis absorption and excitation spectra of the prepared CDs. The aqueous CDs solution clearly emitted bright green-yellow fluorescence with good temperature responsive property. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increasing temperature in the conventional large temperature range of 10 °C–90 °C. Fig. 1(c and d) present fluorescence spectra of the aqueous CDs solution with the environmental temperature rising from 10 °C to 90 °C and decreasing from 90 °C to 10 °C, respectively. The fluorescence intensity of CDs exhibits a sensitive temperature response during the gradual increase and decrease of the environmental temperature. The change of the fluorescent intensity of the prepared CDs in water under five reversible temperature changes, alternating between 20 °C and 80 °C, is depicted in Fig. 1 (e). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs showed good temperature reversibility during the five temperature cycles. The tests demonstrate that the prepared CDs possessed excellent responsive and reversible fluorescence properties to a large range of temperatures spanning from 10 °C to 90 °C. The mechanism of the temperature-sensitive fluorescence of the CDs follows a Boltzmann distribution: with increasing temperature, the collision frequency and non-radiative transition rate of molecules increase while the radiative transition rate remains unchanged [7]. Therefore, the emission intensity of the excited state as well as the fluorescence intensity decrease.

According to the above temperature-sensitive fluorescent characteristics, the prepared CDs aqueous solution of ∼50 mg/mL and PU blended solution (water/glycerol) were mixed at different mass ratios. The weight ratios of CDs to PU were 0.1%, 0.4%, 1%, 2%, and 4%. Then, the CDs with PU mixture was spread on a clean glass substrate. Finally, the PU/CDs composite film was obtained after slowly heating at 50 °C. After evenly drying for 2 h, hybrid PU/CDs composite films with dual-fluorescence emission were obtained. Fig. 2 (a) presents a TEM image of the PU/CDs composite film diluted by one-twentieth (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 2%). Note that the total film formation concentration was diluted by one-twentieth to observe the exact distribution of CDs in the hybrid film. CDs with a size of 2–3 nm were evenly distributed on the PU film. Fig. 2 (b) presents the fluorescence spectra of the corresponding PU film (blue line), CDs (yellow line), and PU/CDs hybrid film (red line). The emission peak of the PU film was close to the blue-light region of ∼417 nm (Ex = 350 nm), and the best emission peak of the CDs was in the green-yellow-light region of ∼552 nm (Ex = 400 nm). The composite film exhibited fluorescence characteristics of dual emission both at ∼415 nm related to PU and at ∼553 nm (Ex = 375 nm) related to the CDs. Fig. 2 (c) presents the transmission spectrum of the PU/CDs composite with a relatively good light transmittance of more than 85% after 550 nm in the optical band. The insert in Fig. 2 (c) shows fluorescent images of the composite films at different stretching states (primitive film, stretching film, and recovery film) under 365 nm UV light at room temperature. To improve the visual effect of the test, the mass ratio of CDs and PU was increased to 8% without affecting the film formation of the PU/CDs composite film. As observed in the insert in Fig. 2 (c), the composite film emits bright yellow-green fluorescence and has good ductility under a certain tension. When the external tensile force disappears, the film primarily returns to its initial state, indicating its good elasticity. Additionally, optical images of the corresponding film collected in bright-field and fluorescence mode (excitation at 360 nm) are presented in Fig. S2. These images further confirm the uniform CDs distribution of the obtained composite film.

Fluorescence images of the PU/CDs composite films prepared using different CDs to PU mass ratios (PU only, 0.4%, 1%, 2%, and 4%) under 365 nm UV light at room temperature are displayed in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence of the pure PU film was bright blue under UV light. When the weight ratio of CDs to PU was low (0.4%), the fluorescence of the composite film was substantially blue, exhibiting little change compared with the blue fluorescence of the PU film. With increasing CDs content, green-yellow fluorescence overlapped with the blue light of PU, and the color of the composite film gradually transformed to blue-green (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 1% and 2%). When the weight ratio of CDs to PU was increased to 4%, the composite films exhibited obvious green with slight yellow superposition fluorescence. This finding suggests that the fluorescence color of the composite films can be adjusted by changing the original reaction ratio without considering the temperature effect, laying a controllable foundation for the subsequent function of the fluorescence temperature response of the PU/CDs composite films.

The above TEM image, fluorescent spectra, and fluorescence images of the PU/CDs composite films under UV excitation demonstrate the effect of the combination of CDs and PU and will facilitate further research on the properties of the composite films with dual emitting source.

In addition, the fluorescence properties of the prepared composite films at different temperatures were investigated. Fig. S3 and Fig. 4 show the corresponding tests at the low weight ratio of CDs to PU of 0.1% and 0.4%, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), the emissions of both the CDs and PU gradually decreased with increasing temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C. Oppositely, the intensities of the above two emissions gradually increased as the temperature was reduced from 90 °C back to 10 °C, as shown in Fig. 4 (b). Apparently, the blue fluorescence signal of PU is much higher than the fluorescence signal of the CDs, which played a dominant role. The temperature response of the blue fluorescence by applying five heating and cooling cycles at 20 °C and 80 °C were investigated. As observed in Fig. 4 (c), the blue fluorescence intensities of the hybrid PU/CDs composite films in different temperature cycles showed good temperature reversibility. Fig. 4 (d) shows the CIE coordinate changes of the hybrid PU/CDs composite films; their fluorescence color changes in the blue range in the temperature range from 10 °C to 90 °C. The change of chromaticity was small and the color span was narrow and mainly concentrated in the blue area. For lower weight ratio of CDs to PU (0.1%), as shown in Fig. S3, the fluorescence properties of the composite film showed a similar temperature response compared with that observed in Fig. 4. The color of the colorimetric fluorescence moved within the blue color range, and the color change span was very narrow. The corresponding fluorescence images of composite films with CDs to PU ratio of 0.4% under UV excitation of 365 nm at increasing temperatures from 20 °C, 50 °C–80 °C, were shown in Fig. S5. Consistent with the results in Fig. 4, the film exhibited decreasing of single blue light with increasing temperature.

Fig. 5 (a and b) present the fluorescence spectra of the composite PU/CDs film (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 1%) for increasing temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C and decreasing temperature from 90 °C to 10 °C, respectively. Apparently, the fluorescence intensity values of the PU and CDs change at the same level. Interestingly, the fluorescence from CDs displayed a similar temperature response behavior as that observed in Figs. 1 and 4, and Fig. S3, whereas no changes of the blue fluorescence from PU at different temperatures were detected. Therefore, the temperature response of the green-yellow fluorescence was investigated by applying five heating and cooling cycles at 20 °C and 80 °C. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), the green-yellow fluorescence intensities of the hybrid PU/CDs composite films possessed good temperature reversibility. Fig. 4 (d) shows the CIE coordinate changes of the hybrid PU/CDs composite films. It is worth noting that the fluorescent color of the films shifted from blue to green to yellow. The color ratio of the CIE clearly changed, and the color span was large (blue, green, and yellow). This finding is consistent with the increase of the proportion of green-yellow fluorescence compared with that in Fig. 4.

As the reaction amount of CDs to PU increased to 2%, the composite film exhibited sensitive green-yellow emission characteristics to temperature in the range of 10 °C–90 °C. The fluorescence intensity decreased with increasing temperature with reversible response (similar to the observations in Figs. 1 and 5). In contrast, the change of the blue fluorescence intensity from PU was very weak. Accordingly, in Fig. 5 (d), the CIE coordinate changes indicated that the fluorescent color of the dual-emission hybrid composite film mainly changed in the green-yellow range from 10 °C to 90 °C with low color span and change. The fluorescence property results for the CDs to PU weight ratio of 4% are presented in Fig. S4. The blue-light signal of PU at ∼415 nm is very weak, whereas the green-yellow fluorescence and change of CDs are dominant. Correspondingly, the composite film system only exhibits the temperature response and CIE color change of the CDs. The corresponding fluorescence images of composite films with CDs to PU ratio of 2% under UV excitation of 365 nm at increasing temperatures from 20 °C, 50 °C–80 °C, were shown in Fig. S6. Consistent with the results in Fig. 6 , the film exhibited decreasing of single yellow-green light with increasing temperature.

It can be speculated that at low weight ratio of CDs to PU (0.1% and 0.4%), the temperature effect mainly affects the fluorescence emission from PU. The fluorescence change of the CDs is covered and weakened. The fluorescence of the dual-emission film is concentrated in the blue-light area with little color change (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3). As the weight ratio of CDs to PU increases to an appropriate ratio (1%), the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is close to that of PU. With increasing temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs gradually decreases, accompanied by a slight change of the fluorescence intensity of PU. Thus, the fluorescence of the dual-emission film changes with temperature over a large color range (yellow to green to blue), as depicted in Fig. 5. When the weight ratio of CDs to PU is high (2% and 4%), the temperature effect mainly affects the fluorescence emission of the CDs. The fluorescence emission from PU is weakened and covered in the composite film system. As a result, the fluorescence color of the dual-emission PU/CDs film moves in the green-yellow-light area (Fig. 6 and Fig. S4). On the basis of the above test results, an appropriate ratio (weight ratio of CDs to PU of 1%) was selected to prepare a PU/CDs composite film. The film exhibited ideal fluorescence dual-emission response properties to temperature (10 °C–90 °C). The proportion of CIE color changed obviously over a large span. The emission color of the film moves in the blue, green, and yellow range with more sensitivity and accuracy with increasing temperature.

Subsequently, the temperature response properties of the PU/CDs composite film with the appropriate weight ratio of CDs to PU of 1% were examined from 10 °C to 90 °C under 365 nm UV light (Fig. 7 ). With increasing temperature, the dual emission color of the composite film gradually changed from yellow-green (yellow dominant, 10 °C) to green (30 °C) to blue-green (50 °C) to blue-green (blue dominant, 70 °C) to blue (90 °C) at the macroscopic visible range. These results further demonstrate that the prepared composite film exhibited good temperature response properties that can be used for macro colorimetric visual detection over a wide temperature range (10 °C–90 °C). This finding implies that the prepared PU/CDs composite film shows great potential for both macro temperature-sensing applications and PU composite development.

Hybrid PU/CDs composite films with dual-light emission were prepared through the uniform mixing of blue fluorescent PU and green-yellow fluorescent CDs. The distribution of CDs on the composite film was relatively uniform. The composite film exhibited relatively good light transmittance of more than 85% after 550 nm in the optical band. In addition, the prepared film exhibited good flexibility, ductility, and mechanical stability. The green-yellow emission of the prepared CDs showed good temperature response and reversible properties; moreover, the obtained dual-emission PU/CDs hybrid films exhibited excellent fluorescence temperature response and reversible properties. By increasing the ratio of CDs to PU, the green-yellow fluorescence of the PU/CDs composite films decreased gradually while their blue fluorescence remained unchanged with increasing temperature. Interestingly, the fluorescence color of the composite film could be adjusted by changing the original reaction ratio of CDs to PU without considering other effects. Moreover, at the selected reaction ratio of CDs to PU, the dual-emission color of the composite film gradually changed from yellow-green (yellow dominant, 10 °C) to green (30 °C) to blue-green (50 °C) to blue-green (blue dominant, 70 °C) to blue (90 °C) at the macroscopic visible range. The obtained PU/CDs hybrid films with dual emission also exhibited excellent temperature response and reversible properties over the large temperature range from 10 °C to 90 °C. Thus, the stretchable hybrid PU/CDs composite films could potentially be applied for colorimetric detection over a large temperature range (10 °C–90 °C). These materials thus possess high application value for macroscopic dual-light temperature sensing. In addition, the strong interaction between the waterborne PU matrix and CDs will greatly promote the construction and design of new diverse sensing PU composites.

Yu Zhao and Xiaojie Zhang performed Conceptualization and Supervision; Yingnan Jiang, Yunxiao Jia, Lizhi Xiao, Ruyue Yan and Jingwei Xin performed original draft, writing, review & editing; Chunxia Yin, Chunying Xiao, Zhe Zhang and Wei Song performed Funding acquisition and Resources.

Yingnan Jiang: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Xiaojie Zhang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Lizhi Xiao: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Ruyue Yan: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Jingwei Xin: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Chunxia Yin: Funding acquisition, Resources. Yunxiao Jia: Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Yu Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision. Chunying Xiao: Funding acquisition, Resources. Zhe Zhang: Funding acquisition, Resources. Wei Song: Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank for the support from Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, Chemistry and Materials Engineering College, Jiang Nan University. This work was supported by Jilin Province science and technology development plan (Grant No. 20180201060SF), Ministry of science and technology (Grant No. 2018YFC1706603), Science and technology development project of Jilin Province (Grant No. 20170309007YY), Jilin Provincial health special project (No. sczsy201605), Jilin Province bureau of quality and technical supervision project (DBXM146-2016, DBXM109-2017, DBXM114-2017, DBXM099-2019), and excellent talents training program of China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University (No. YXQ1-201906).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2020.03.013.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Childs PRN, Greenwood JR, Long CA. Review of temperature measurement. Rev Sci Instrum 2000;71(8):2959-2978.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrahigh-sensitive optical temperature sensing based on quasi-thermalized green emissions from Er: ZnO
